messages.content:
## Question

Explain gravity to a child.

response_text:
Gravity is an invisible pull that tries to bring things together. The Earth is very big, so its pull is strong and keeps you, your toys, and even the air close to the ground. That’s why when you jump up, you come back down, and why an apple falls from a tree instead of floating away.

Big things pull harder, and things that are closer feel the pull more. That’s why you would weigh less on the Moon (it’s smaller, so it pulls less) and more on a bigger planet. Your “weight” is just how hard Earth’s gravity is pulling on you.

Gravity doesn’t need air to work—it pulls in space, too. Astronauts look like they’re floating because their spaceship and their bodies are falling around Earth together, so they don’t feel the ground pushing up on them.

Try this:
- Drop two toys at the same time from the same height. If they’re about the same shape, they’ll hit the ground together—gravity pulls on them the same way.
- Jump straight up and notice you always come back down. That’s gravity saying, “Hey, come back!”
